refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable execution of the load→travel→dump cycle within the customer-defined cycle time target.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) supporting/enabling the capability to achieve cycle time, and (2) actually achieving the measured KPI. The refined FR is atomic and solution-neutral by focusing on the functional obligation—enabling the cycle to execute within the time envelope—without prescribing how (e.g., via speed optimization, path planning, operator training, or equipment design). The actual achievement of p95 ≤ CT_target is a verification criterion, not a functional requirement. This separation allows DPs to address enablement independently of operational performance measurement.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that under nominal duty cycle and site conditions, the NGHE solution's design permits completion of load→travel→dump within CT_target. Measure p95 cycle time across representative worksite trials; if p95 ≤ CT_target, the FR is satisfied. If p95 > CT_target, investigate whether the failure is due to (a) inadequate DP (design cannot support the target) or (b) operational/environmental factors outside the DP scope.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-002.1: The NGHE solution shall enable the operator to load material within the defined load time envelope.
FR-NGHE-002.2: The NGHE solution shall enable the operator to travel from load site to dump site within the defined travel time envelope.
FR-NGHE-002.3: The NGHE solution shall enable the operator to dump material within the defined dump time envelope.

candidate_dps: DP1: Decompose cycle into discrete, sequenced phases (load, travel, dump) with phase-gating logic that enforces strict ordering and allows independent optimization of each phase duration.
DP2: Implement a centralized cycle-time controller that monitors elapsed time against phase budgets and dynamically adjusts operational parameters (speed, acceleration, dwell) to meet p95 target.

DP3: Design modular subsystem interfaces (propulsion, hydraulics, control) such that each subsystem exposes cycle-phase-relevant performance metrics (e.g., load-to-motion latency, travel speed capability, dump duration) enabling predictable composition.

DP4: Establish a duty-cycle characterization model (payload mass distribution, terrain profile, ambient conditions) and validate cycle-time performance offline before field deployment, with on-site tuning gates.
DP5: Implement redundant or fail-safe timing mechanisms (watchdog timers, phase-timeout handlers) to ensure cycle integrity and prevent indefinite phase hangs that would violate p95 acceptance.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP1 + DP3 + DP4 (integrated approach): Decompose the load→travel→dump cycle into independently optimizable phases with well-defined subsystem interfaces that expose cycle-relevant performance metrics. Validate the composed cycle-time model against duty-cycle characterization and site conditions before deployment. This approach decouples phase logic from subsystem implementation details, supports modular testing, and provides a clear acceptance-criteria bridge.

potential_sources: Phase-gating logic must couple to propulsion subsystem state machine to ensure load phase completes before travel initiates—mitigated by explicit interface contract (e.g., 'load-complete' signal).

Cycle-time budget allocation across phases depends on subsystem capability (e.g., max travel speed, dump hydraulic flow)—mitigated by DP4 characterization model that captures these dependencies upfront.
Site conditions (terrain, payload variance) introduce runtime variability—mitigated by p95 acceptance criterion and on-site tuning gates that allow parameter adjustment without redesign.
DP5 (timing redundancy) introduces secondary control logic—acceptable coupling if watchdog/timeout handlers are stateless and decoupled from primary phase logic.
coupling_check.notes: The selected DP avoids tight coupling by establishing clear phase boundaries and subsystem interface contracts. Variability is managed through characterization and acceptance testing rather than embedded in control logic. Risk of coupling is low if phase-gating and subsystem interfaces are treated as architectural invariants.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Subsystem performance (propulsion speed, hydraulic flow, load-handling latency) is repeatable and measurable under controlled conditions. RISK: If subsystem performance is highly variable or degraded over time, p95 target may not be achievable without active feedback control or predictive maintenance.

ASSUMPTION: Site conditions (terrain, payload mass, ambient temperature) can be characterized and validated before deployment. RISK: If site conditions are unpredictable or change during operation, offline validation may not reflect field performance; mitigation requires on-site tuning and monitoring.

ASSUMPTION: Phase-gating logic can be implemented with low latency and high reliability (no race conditions, deadlocks). RISK: If control system has high jitter or unreliable state transitions, phase timing may be unpredictable; mitigation requires real-time OS or deterministic scheduler.

ASSUMPTION: Customer-defined cycle-time target is technically feasible given subsystem capabilities and site constraints. RISK: If target is infeasible (e.g., requires travel speed exceeding safe limits), acceptance criteria cannot be met; mitigation requires early feasibility review with customer.

ASSUMPTION: p95 acceptance criterion is sufficient to capture customer satisfaction and operational requirements. RISK: If customer has unstated requirements (e.g., p99 or mean cycle time), acceptance may fail despite meeting p95; mitigation requires explicit acceptance-criteria negotiation.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly state: (1) the duty cycle characterization (payload range, terrain profile, ambient conditions) that defines the acceptance test environment; (2) the p95 measurement protocol (number of cycles, sampling method, exclusion criteria for anomalies); (3) the on-site tuning and acceptance gates (e.g., 'cycle time shall be validated over 100 consecutive cycles under representative duty before handoff'). This revision bridges the gap between the logical DP (phase decomposition and subsystem interfaces) and the measurable acceptance criteria, reducing ambiguity in IM realization and test planning.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Integrated cycle decomposition, phase orchestration, performance metrics aggregation, and duty-cycle validation logic that bridges subsystem interfaces to cycle-time acceptance criteria.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives phase-level performance data (load mass, travel distance, dump completion status, cycle timestamps) from Vehicle subsystems to populate cycle-time model.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Sends phase-sequencing commands and performance targets to Vehicle subsystems; enforces phase boundaries and cycle logic.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives safety constraints that may alter phase timing; broadcasts cycle-state alerts if duty-cycle validation fails or acceptance criteria are at risk.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Transmits cycle-time model validation results and site-condition adjustments; receives updated environmental/operational parameters for re-validation.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: cycle decomposition, phase orchestration, and duty-cycle validation are core NGHE responsibilities. Phase logic is decoupled from subsystem implementation (Vehicle handles actuation). Minimal diffusion: only telemetry ingestion and tasking egress cross boundaries; validation logic remains internal.

Poor fit: Vehicle owns subsystem implementations (load, travel, dump mechanisms) but should not own cycle orchestration or duty-cycle validation logic. Placing DP here couples phase logic tightly to hardware, violates decoupling principle, and makes modular testing difficult. Cycle-time model would diffuse across multiple subsystem drivers.

Very poor fit: Teleoperation is a control interface layer, not a cycle-logic owner. Placing cycle decomposition and validation here conflates operator command sequencing with autonomous cycle orchestration. High diffusion: would require tight coupling to Vehicle subsystems and Safety Services.

Poor fit: Safety Services owns constraint enforcement and alert generation, not cycle orchestration. Placing DP here makes Safety Services responsible for performance optimization and duty-cycle validation, which is outside its domain. Would require extensive coupling to Vehicle and Teleoperation.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The NGHE Solution module is the natural owner of integrated cycle decomposition, phase orchestration, and duty-cycle validation because: (1) it sits at the system-level orchestration layer above subsystem implementations; (2) it can decompose the load→travel→dump cycle into independently optimizable phases without being constrained by hardware details; (3) it can aggregate telemetry from Vehicle subsystems and validate the composed cycle-time model against site conditions; (4) it can expose cycle-relevant performance metrics through well-defined interfaces (Telemetry in, Tasking out) that decouple phase logic from subsystem implementation; (5) it provides a clear acceptance-criteria bridge by validating the model before deployment and monitoring compliance during operation.
containment_risks: Risk: NGHE Solution may lack direct access to real-time subsystem state if Vehicle telemetry is coarse-grained. Mitigation: define Telemetry interface to include phase-level metrics (load mass, travel distance, dump status, timestamps) with sufficient granularity for cycle-time model validation.

Risk: Cycle-time model validation may require site-specific parameters (terrain, payload distribution, ambient conditions) that are not available at design time. Mitigation: design NGHE Solution to accept site-condition inputs via Network Link and re-validate the model before deployment; document acceptance criteria as a function of site parameters.

Risk: Safety constraints may conflict with phase-optimization logic, causing validation failures. Mitigation: SafetyAlert interface must be bidirectional; NGHE Solution must incorporate safety constraints into the cycle-time model and alert operators if duty-cycle targets cannot be met safely.

Risk: Teleoperation may override phase sequencing, breaking the decoupled cycle logic. Mitigation: define clear handoff rules: Teleoperation can request cycle start/stop, but NGHE Solution owns phase transitions and timing.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: No revision to DP choice is necessary. The selected DP (DP1 + DP3 + DP4) is well-suited to NGHE Solution ownership. However, strengthen the DP specification by explicitly requiring: (1) a formal cycle-time model that decomposes load→travel→dump into phases with measurable entry/exit conditions; (2) a validation protocol that checks the model against duty-cycle characterization and site conditions before deployment; (3) a metrics interface (Telemetry) that exposes phase-level performance data (not just raw sensor readings) to enable model validation; (4) a tasking interface (Tasking) that enforces phase boundaries and prevents subsystem implementations from deviating from the orchestrated cycle. This ensures the DP remains decoupled from Vehicle implementation details while providing a clear acceptance-criteria bridge.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The FR acceptance criterion (p95 cycle time ≤ CT_target under agreed duty cycle and site conditions) is directly measurable through the NGHE Solution's Telemetry interface (phase-level performance data: load mass, travel distance, dump completion status, cycle timestamps). The IM boundary explicitly includes duty-cycle validation logic and performance metrics aggregation, enabling pre-deployment model validation and runtime compliance monitoring. Site-condition re-validation via Network Link ensures acceptance criteria are contextualized to actual operational parameters.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Excellent. The FR→DP→IM chain is clear: FR (enable cycle execution within target time) maps to DP (decompose cycle into independently optimizable phases with well-defined subsystem interfaces and duty-cycle validation) and is owned by NGHE Solution IM (cycle orchestration, phase sequencing, metrics aggregation, validation logic). The IM boundary explicitly excludes subsystem actuation (owned by Vehicle) and operator command sequencing (owned by Teleoperation), maintaining clean separation of concerns. Acceptance criteria bridge is explicit: NGHE Solution validates the cycle-time model against duty-cycle characterization and site conditions before deployment, then monitors p95 compliance during operation.

diffusion_risks: Risk: Phase-level telemetry granularity. If Vehicle subsystems expose only coarse-grained sensor data (e.g., raw load cell readings, GPS coordinates) rather than phase-relevant metrics (load mass, travel distance, dump status, phase timestamps), NGHE Solution will be forced to implement phase-state inference logic, diffusing the DP across Vehicle and NGHE boundaries. Mitigation: Telemetry interface specification must mandate phase-level metric exposure (not raw sensor data) from Vehicle subsystems; define clear contracts for load mass accuracy, travel distance measurement, dump completion detection, and timestamp synchronization.

Risk: Cycle-time model validation logic may leak into Vehicle subsystem drivers if subsystems lack sufficient autonomy to execute phase commands without real-time guidance. If Vehicle subsystems require continuous cycle-time feedback to adjust actuation timing, the DP (cycle decomposition and validation) will diffuse across Vehicle implementation. Mitigation: Tasking interface must define phase commands with clear entry/exit conditions and performance targets; Vehicle subsystems must be capable of executing phases autonomously within specified tolerances; NGHE Solution validates post-hoc, not in-loop.

Risk: Safety constraint integration. If SafetyAlert interface is unidirectional (Safety Services → NGHE Solution only), NGHE Solution cannot communicate back when duty-cycle validation fails due to safety constraints, creating a feedback loop that may require manual intervention. This could diffuse cycle-time model adjustment logic into Safety Services or Teleoperation. Mitigation: SafetyAlert must be bidirectional; NGHE Solution must be able to query safety constraints during model validation and alert Safety Services if cycle targets cannot be met safely.

Risk: Site-condition parameter management. If site-specific parameters (terrain, payload distribution, ambient conditions) are not systematically managed through the Network Link interface, NGHE Solution may be forced to hard-code assumptions or accept manual parameter entry, diffusing validation logic across deployment workflows. Mitigation: Network Link interface must support structured site-condition input and re-validation triggers; document which parameters require re-validation and which are fixed at design time.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment (0.92 score). NGHE Solution is the natural owner of integrated cycle decomposition, phase orchestration, and duty-cycle validation. The IM boundary is well-defined: it owns cycle logic, phase sequencing, metrics aggregation, and validation; it does not own subsystem actuation (Vehicle), operator control (Teleoperation), or constraint enforcement (Safety Services). The four external interfaces (Telemetry in, Tasking out, SafetyAlert inout, Network Link inout) are minimal and well-scoped, enabling NGHE Solution to remain decoupled from subsystem implementation details while providing a clear acceptance-criteria bridge. Minimal diffusion (0.08 score) is acceptable: only telemetry ingestion and tasking egress cross boundaries; validation logic remains internal. However, the four diffusion risks above must be mitigated through interface specification and subsystem capability requirements to prevent DP leakage during implementation.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The DP (DP1 + DP3 + DP4: integrated cycle decomposition, phase orchestration, duty-cycle validation, and site-condition re-validation) directly addresses the FR (enable load→travel→dump cycle execution within customer-defined cycle time target). The DP is solution-neutral (does not prescribe specific algorithms or hardware), logically sound (decomposes a complex cycle into independently optimizable phases with well-defined interfaces), and verifiable (cycle-time model can be validated against duty-cycle characterization and site conditions before deployment, and p95 compliance can be monitored at runtime). The DP explicitly bridges to acceptance criteria by requiring duty-cycle validation and site-condition contextualization, ensuring the cycle-time target is achievable and measurable under agreed operational conditions. No revision to DP choice is necessary.

recommended_revisions: Strengthen Telemetry interface specification: mandate that Vehicle subsystems expose phase-level performance metrics (load mass [kg], travel distance [m], dump completion status [boolean], phase timestamps [UTC]) rather than raw sensor data. Define accuracy and latency requirements for each metric to enable reliable cycle-time model validation.

Formalize the cycle-time model specification: document the decomposition of load→travel→dump into phases with measurable entry/exit conditions, phase-duration distributions (mean, p95, p99), and dependencies. Define how the model is parameterized by site conditions (terrain type, payload distribution, ambient temperature, etc.) and which parameters require re-validation before deployment.

Define the duty-cycle validation protocol: specify the acceptance test procedure (e.g., N repeated cycles under specified site conditions, measurement of p95 cycle time, comparison to CT_target), the pass/fail criteria, and the re-validation trigger conditions (e.g., site-condition changes exceeding specified thresholds). Document how validation results are communicated to operators and how failures are escalated.

Clarify Tasking interface semantics: define phase commands (e.g., 'Load', 'Travel', 'Dump') with explicit entry/exit conditions, performance targets (e.g., load mass tolerance, travel distance tolerance, dump completion timeout), and error handling (e.g., what happens if a phase times out or fails). Specify that Vehicle subsystems execute phases autonomously and report completion status back via Telemetry.

Establish SafetyAlert bidirectionality: define the protocol for NGHE Solution to query safety constraints during cycle-time model validation (e.g., 'Can phase X be executed with duration Y under current safety constraints?') and for NGHE Solution to alert Safety Services if duty-cycle targets cannot be met safely. Document escalation procedures.

Implement site-condition management via Network Link: define a structured format for site-condition inputs (terrain type, payload distribution, ambient conditions, etc.), a re-validation trigger mechanism (e.g., 're-validate if terrain type changes'), and a versioning scheme for site-condition parameters. Document which parameters are fixed at design time and which are variable.

Add subsystem capability requirements: specify that Vehicle subsystems must be capable of executing load, travel, and dump phases autonomously within specified tolerances (e.g., ±5% travel distance, ±10 s dump completion time) without real-time guidance from NGHE Solution. This ensures the DP remains decoupled from subsystem implementation details.

Document acceptance-criteria traceability: create a traceability matrix linking the FR (p95 cycle time ≤ CT_target) to the DP (cycle decomposition, phase orchestration, duty-cycle validation) to the IM (NGHE Solution module with Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link interfaces). Specify how each interface contributes to acceptance-criteria verification.

decision.justification: Gate results confirm FR→DP→IM chain is sound (all three gates pass with high confidence). However, the recommended_revisions and diffusion_risks reveal that the current DP specification is insufficiently detailed to guide safe, unambiguous implementation. The four diffusion risks (telemetry granularity, subsystem autonomy, safety bidirectionality, site-condition management) are not showstoppers but require explicit sub-FR decomposition to ensure they are addressed systematically during design rather than discovered during implementation. Given high safety criticality, deriving next-level sub-FRs to formalize interface contracts, validation protocols, and subsystem capability requirements is justified. This prevents DP leakage and ensures acceptance criteria remain verifiable and traceable through implementation.
next_level_sub_frs: FR1.1: Expose phase-level performance metrics (load mass, travel distance, dump completion status, phase timestamps) from Vehicle subsystems via Telemetry interface with specified accuracy and latency to enable cycle-time model validation.

FR1.2: Decompose load→travel→dump cycle into independently executable phases with measurable entry/exit conditions, phase-duration distributions, and site-condition parameterization to enable duty-cycle validation before deployment.

FR1.3: Validate cycle-time model against duty-cycle characterization (N repeated cycles under specified site conditions, p95 measurement, comparison to CT_target) and re-validate when site conditions change beyond specified thresholds.

FR1.4: Execute phase commands (Load, Travel, Dump) autonomously within Vehicle subsystems with specified tolerances (e.g., ±5% travel distance, ±10 s dump completion) without real-time guidance from NGHE Solution.
FR1.5: Query safety constraints during cycle-time model validation and alert Safety Services bidirectionally if duty-cycle targets cannot be met safely.

FR1.6: Manage site-condition parameters (terrain type, payload distribution, ambient conditions) via Network Link with structured input format, re-validation triggers, and versioning to contextualize cycle-time targets to actual operational conditions.

revise.why: The current DP (DP1 + DP3 + DP4) is logically sound but lacks sufficient specification detail to prevent the four identified diffusion risks during implementation. Specifically: (1) Telemetry interface must mandate phase-level metrics, not raw sensor data; (2) Cycle-time model must be formally decomposed with entry/exit conditions and site-condition parameterization; (3) Duty-cycle validation protocol must be explicit (test procedure, pass/fail criteria, re-validation triggers); (4) Tasking interface must define phase command semantics and subsystem autonomy requirements; (5) SafetyAlert must be bidirectional; (6) Network Link must support structured site-condition management. These refinements should be captured as sub-level DPs derived from the next-level sub-FRs to ensure the DP remains solution-neutral while providing sufficient guidance to prevent implementation ambiguity and DP leakage.

assumptions: Vehicle subsystems are capable of executing load, travel, and dump phases autonomously within specified tolerances without real-time guidance from NGHE Solution.

Telemetry interface can be extended to expose phase-level metrics (load mass, travel distance, dump completion status, phase timestamps) with accuracy and latency suitable for cycle-time model validation.
SafetyAlert interface can be made bidirectional to support constraint queries and failure alerts from NGHE Solution.
Network Link interface can support structured site-condition input and re-validation triggers.
Duty-cycle validation can be performed pre-deployment through repeated cycle execution under specified site conditions with p95 measurement.
Site-condition parameters can be systematically managed and versioned to enable re-validation when operational context changes.
open_risks: Telemetry granularity risk: If Vehicle subsystems expose only raw sensor data (load cell readings, GPS coordinates) rather than phase-relevant metrics, NGHE Solution will be forced to implement phase-state inference logic, diffusing the DP. Mitigation: Telemetry interface specification must mandate phase-level metric exposure with defined accuracy and latency.

Subsystem autonomy risk: If Vehicle subsystems require continuous cycle-time feedback to adjust actuation timing, the DP will diffuse across Vehicle implementation. Mitigation: Tasking interface must define phase commands with clear entry/exit conditions; Vehicle subsystems must execute phases autonomously within specified tolerances.

Safety bidirectionality risk: If SafetyAlert is unidirectional, NGHE Solution cannot query constraints during validation or alert Safety Services of failures, creating feedback loops that may diffuse logic into Safety Services or Teleoperation. Mitigation: SafetyAlert must be bidirectional with explicit query/alert protocols.

Site-condition management risk: If site-specific parameters are not systematically managed, NGHE Solution may hard-code assumptions or accept manual entry, diffusing validation logic across deployment workflows. Mitigation: Network Link must support structured site-condition input with re-validation triggers.

Acceptance-criteria traceability risk: If the link between FR (p95 cycle time ≤ CT_target), DP (cycle decomposition, phase orchestration, duty-cycle validation), and IM (NGHE Solution module) is not explicitly documented, verification may be incomplete or ambiguous. Mitigation: Create traceability matrix linking FR→DP→IM with interface contributions to acceptance-criteria verification.
evidence_needed: Telemetry interface specification document defining phase-level metrics (load mass [kg], travel distance [m], dump completion status [boolean], phase timestamps [UTC]) with accuracy requirements (e.g., ±2% load mass, ±1 m travel distance, ±100 ms timestamp) and latency requirements (e.g., <500 ms metric delivery).

Cycle-time model specification document decomposing load→travel→dump into phases with measurable entry/exit conditions, phase-duration distributions (mean, p95, p99), and site-condition parameterization (terrain type, payload distribution, ambient temperature, etc.).

Duty-cycle validation protocol specification defining acceptance test procedure (N repeated cycles, p95 measurement, comparison to CT_target), pass/fail criteria, and re-validation trigger conditions (e.g., terrain type change, payload distribution change exceeding ±10%).

Tasking interface specification defining phase commands (Load, Travel, Dump) with entry/exit conditions, performance targets (load mass tolerance, travel distance tolerance, dump completion timeout), and error handling (timeout, failure escalation).

SafetyAlert bidirectionality protocol specification defining constraint query format (e.g., 'Can phase X be executed with duration Y under current safety constraints?'), alert format (e.g., 'Duty-cycle target cannot be met safely due to constraint Z'), and escalation procedures.

Network Link site-condition management specification defining structured input format (JSON schema or equivalent), re-validation trigger mechanism, versioning scheme, and documentation of fixed vs. variable parameters.

Subsystem capability requirements specification defining Vehicle subsystem autonomy requirements (e.g., execute load phase within ±5% tolerance, travel phase within ±5% distance tolerance, dump phase within ±10 s completion time) without real-time guidance.

Acceptance-criteria traceability matrix linking FR (p95 cycle time ≤ CT_target under agreed duty cycle and site conditions) to DP (cycle decomposition, phase orchestration, duty-cycle validation, site-condition re-validation) to IM (NGHE Solution module with Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link interfaces) with explicit interface contributions to verification.
